this reason that members of the
l. w. w. will not go on the street
to speak in the night. They fear
that the detectives, who, they
claim. are hired by employers to
bring public sentiment to conflict
with the laboring men, will take
advantage of such opportunities to
throw rocks and to start riots
which would be charged against
the l. w. w.


Reports from the emigration of
ficials In Portland, Ore, who have
been in Spokane during the trou
BILE, say that the government spies
have found many men who have
taken out their first naturalization
papers in the United States parti
cipating in the fight. The law
makes such men liable to deporta.
ton and, at least, participation in
breach Of law lays a predicate for
the federal authorities refusal to
give them final papers.


Even when the free speech fight
is over the trouble between the of.
ficials and the authorities will
scarcely be at an end. Vincent
st. John, general counsel of the /
w. W., has directed that all aliens
cited for deportation shall be in.
structed to return to Spokane at
all hazard. This would indicate
that trouble may be made between
the United States and the aliens
respective countries because of de-
portations.
